mission.title: Install and Configure Tokenetes on Kubernetes
mission.description: Tokenetes is an open source Transaction Tokens (TraTs) Service that allows for the management and deployment of transaction tokens. Installing Tokenetes enables you to utilize its features for handling transaction tokens in your applications.

steps.title: Add the Tokenetes Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Tokenetes Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration:
```bash
helm repo add tokenetes https://charts.tokenetes.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Tokenetes using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install the Tokenetes service using Helm. This command will create a namespace called 'tokenetes' and deploy the service:
```bash
helm install tokenetes tokenetes/tokenetes --version v1.0.1 --namespace tokenetes --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Tokenetes pods are running correctly:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace tokenetes
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure that the Tokenetes service has appropriate resource limits, you can edit the deployment:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment tokenetes --namespace tokenetes
```
In the editor, add resource limits under the container spec:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"
```

steps.title: Access the Tokenetes service
steps.description:
To access the Tokenetes service, you can set up port forwarding:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/tokenetes --namespace tokenetes 8080:80
```
You can now access the service at http://localhost:8080.

resolution.summary: A successful installation will show the Tokenetes pods in a running state when you execute `kubectl get pods --namespace tokenetes`. You will also be able to access the service via port forwarding at http://localhost:8080.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add tokenetes https://charts.tokenetes.io
helm repo update
helm install tokenetes tokenetes/tokenetes --version v1.0.1 --namespace tokenetes --create-namespace

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the Tokenetes release, use the following command to uninstall it from the cluster:
```bash
helm uninstall tokenetes --namespace tokenetes
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
After uninstalling the release, you should clean up any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created. Run the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep tokenetes | awk '{print $1}')
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace tokenetes
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, remove the 'tokenetes' namespace and verify that it has been deleted:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace tokenetes
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up the current state of your Tokenetes installation. You can use the following command to export the current configuration:
```bash
helm get values tokenetes --namespace tokenetes > tokenetes-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Tokenetes release to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade tokenetes tokenetes/tokenetes --version v1.1.0 --namespace tokenetes
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the Tokenetes pods are running the new version:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace tokenetes
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Tokenetes pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for more information:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace tokenetes
``` 
Look for any error messages that could indicate configuration issues or missing dependencies.

troubleshooting.title: Service not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Tokenetes service, ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly. Verify the service status:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace tokenetes
``` 
If the service is not running, check the deployment for issues.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter errors related to insufficient resources, check the resource limits and requests set for the deployment:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment tokenetes --namespace tokenetes
``` 
Adjust the resource limits in the deployment if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade fails, check for any error messages in the command output. You can also view the current release status:
```bash
helm status tokenetes --namespace tokenetes
``` 
If needed, you can rollback to the previous version using:
```bash
helm rollback tokenetes <revision-number> --namespace tokenetes
```

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
